quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 7 Quiz 6
quiz.subtitle: Geometric Mean and Altitude to Hypotenuse Theorems
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: When the altitude is drawn to the hypotenuse of a right triangle, how many similar triangles are formed?
questions.explanation: The altitude to the hypotenuse divides the original right triangle into two smaller right triangles. All three triangles (the original and the two smaller ones) are similar to each other by AA similarity.

questions.question: The altitude to the hypotenuse is the geometric mean between:
options: The two segments of the hypotenuse
The two legs of the original triangle
questions.explanation: The altitude to the hypotenuse is the geometric mean between the two segments of the hypotenuse. If the segments are $x$ and $y$, then altitude $h = \sqrt{x \cdot y}$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle ABC$ with right angle at $C$, altitude $\overline{CD}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{AB}$. If $AD = 4$ and $DB = 9$, what is $CD$?
questions.explanation: The altitude is the geometric mean of the segments: $CD = \sqrt{AD \cdot DB} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 9} = \sqrt{36} = 6$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle ABC$ with right angle at $C$, altitude $\overline{CD}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{AB}$. Each leg of the original triangle is the geometric mean between:
options: The full hypotenuse and the adjacent segment of the hypotenuse
The two distinct segments formed by the altitude on the hypotenuse
The altitude measurement and the complete hypotenuse length
The opposite leg measurement and the corresponding altitude
questions.explanation: Each leg is the geometric mean between the hypotenuse and the segment of the hypotenuse adjacent to that leg. For leg $AC$: $AC = \sqrt{AB \cdot AD}$. For leg $BC$: $BC = \sqrt{AB \cdot DB}$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle PQR$ with right angle at $R$, altitude $\overline{RS}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{PQ}$. If $PS = 3$ and $PQ = 12$, what is $PR$?
questions.explanation: Leg $PR$ is adjacent to segment $PS$. Using the leg rule: $PR = \sqrt{PQ \cdot PS} = \sqrt{12 \cdot 3} = \sqrt{36} = 6$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle PQR$ with right angle at $R$, altitude $\overline{RS}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{PQ}$. If $PS = 3$, $SQ = 9$, and $PQ = 12$, what is $QR$?
questions.explanation: Leg $QR$ is adjacent to segment $SQ$. Using the leg rule: $QR = \sqrt{PQ \cdot SQ} = \sqrt{12 \cdot 9} = \sqrt{108} = \sqrt{36 \cdot 3} = 6\sqrt{3}$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle XYZ$ with right angle at $Z$, altitude $\overline{ZW}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{XY}$. If $XW = 5$ and $WY = 20$, what is $XZ$?
questions.explanation: First, $XY = XW + WY = 5 + 20 = 25$. Leg $XZ$ is adjacent to $XW$: $XZ = \sqrt{XY \cdot XW} = \sqrt{25 \cdot 5} = \sqrt{125} = 5\sqrt{5}$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle ABC$ with right angle at $C$, altitude $\overline{CD}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{AB}$. If $AD = 2$ and $CD = 6$, what is $DB$?
questions.explanation: Using the altitude rule: $CD = \sqrt{AD \cdot DB}$, so $6 = \sqrt{2 \cdot DB}$. Squaring both sides: $36 = 2 \cdot DB$, giving $DB = 18$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle DEF$ with right angle at $F$, altitude $\overline{FG}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{DE}$. If $DG = 4$ and $GE = 16$, find the length of altitude $FG$.
questions.explanation: The altitude is the geometric mean of the two segments: $FG = \sqrt{DG \cdot GE} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 16} = \sqrt{64} = 8$.

questions.question: In right $\triangle JKL$ with right angle at $L$, altitude $\overline{LM}$ is drawn to hypotenuse $\overline{JK}$. If $JM = 3$ and $JK = 27$, what is $JL$?
questions.explanation: Leg $JL$ is adjacent to segment $JM$: $JL = \sqrt{JK \cdot JM} = \sqrt{27 \cdot 3} = \sqrt{81} = 9$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the geometric mean relationships in right triangles — the altitude rule and the leg rule.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the three key relationships: altitude = $\sqrt{seg_1 \cdot seg_2}$, and each leg = $\sqrt{hypotenuse \cdot adjacent\_segment}$.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on identifying which segments to use: the altitude relates to both hypotenuse segments; each leg relates to the whole hypotenuse and the segment adjacent to that leg.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Remember: when the altitude meets the hypotenuse, three geometric mean relationships emerge — one for the altitude and one for each leg. Set up the correct proportion and solve.
